Hippocampus_L.summary: Critical hub for memory formation and spatial navigation in the medial temporal lobe.
Hippocampus_L.function: Converts short-term memories into long-term memories through a process called consolidation. Essential for episodic memory (personal experiences) and spatial memory (mental maps). Works with the entorhinal cortex to process and store new information.
Hippocampus_L.location: Located in the medial temporal lobe, curved like a seahorse (hence the name, from Greek 'hippokampos'). Lies beneath the cerebral cortex, adjacent to the amygdala.
Hippocampus_L.clinical: Damage causes anterograde amnesia (inability to form new memories). One of the first regions affected in Alzheimer's disease. Chronic stress can cause hippocampal atrophy, linked to depression.
Hippocampus_L.funFact: One of only two brain regions known to generate new neurons in adults (neurogenesis), making it uniquely plastic throughout life.

Hippocampus_R.summary: Right hemisphere hub for spatial memory and scene construction.
Hippocampus_R.function: Specializes in spatial memory and navigation. Helps construct mental scenes and imagines future scenarios. Works together with the left hippocampus but shows specialization for non-verbal, spatial information.
Hippocampus_R.location: Located in the right medial temporal lobe, mirroring the left hippocampus. Part of the limbic system.
Hippocampus_R.clinical: Damage can impair spatial navigation and memory for places. London taxi drivers famously show enlarged right posterior hippocampi due to extensive navigation training.
Hippocampus_R.funFact: The right hippocampus is particularly active when you're imagining yourself in a different place or time.

Amygdala_L.summary: Emotional processing center specializing in fear and threat detection.
Amygdala_L.function: Processes emotional significance of stimuli, especially fear and threat. Modulates memory consolidation for emotionally arousing events. Involved in social behavior and reading facial expressions.
Amygdala_L.location: Almond-shaped structure (amygdala means 'almond' in Greek) in the medial temporal lobe, anterior to the hippocampus.
Amygdala_L.clinical: Hyperactivity linked to anxiety disorders and PTSD. Damage can result in inability to recognize fear in faces. Involved in addiction through its role in emotional memory.
Amygdala_L.funFact: The amygdala can process threat information so fast that you may react before you're consciously aware of danger.

Amygdala_R.summary: Right hemisphere emotional processor with role in implicit emotional processing.
Amygdala_R.function: Processes emotional content, particularly unconscious or rapidly presented emotional stimuli. May be more involved in automatic, unconscious emotional processing compared to the left amygdala.
Amygdala_R.location: Right medial temporal lobe, anterior to the hippocampus, within the limbic system.
Amygdala_R.clinical: Implicated in processing subliminal emotional information. Abnormalities associated with psychopathy and autism spectrum conditions.
Amygdala_R.funFact: The right amygdala responds more to masked (unconsciously perceived) emotional faces.

Precentral_L.summary: Primary motor cortex controlling voluntary movement of the right side of the body.
Precentral_L.function: Executes voluntary movements by sending signals down the corticospinal tract. Contains the motor homunculus - a distorted body map where areas like hands and face have disproportionately large representations.
Precentral_L.location: Precentral gyrus of the frontal lobe, just anterior to the central sulcus. Left hemisphere controls right body.
Precentral_L.clinical: Damage causes contralateral paralysis or weakness (right side of body). Stroke here can cause hemiplegia. Target for brain-computer interfaces.
Precentral_L.funFact: The area for hand control is so large that it takes up about a third of the motor cortex, reflecting our dexterity.

Precentral_R.summary: Primary motor cortex controlling voluntary movement of the left side of the body.
Precentral_R.function: Controls voluntary movements of the left side of the body. Integrates sensory feedback from the postcentral gyrus to refine movements.
Precentral_R.location: Right precentral gyrus, anterior to the central sulcus in the frontal lobe.
Precentral_R.clinical: Lesions cause left-sided weakness or paralysis. Important for left-handed fine motor control.
Precentral_R.funFact: Musicians and athletes show enhanced motor cortex representations for their trained movements.

Insula_L.summary: Hidden cortex involved in interoception, emotion, and awareness.
Insula_L.function: Integrates body sensations (interoception) with emotional states. Involved in taste, disgust, empathy, and self-awareness. Plays role in decision-making and risk assessment.
Insula_L.location: Deep within the lateral sulcus, hidden beneath the frontal, parietal, and temporal opercula. Also called the 'Island of Reil'.
Insula_L.clinical: Damage can cause loss of taste, impaired disgust recognition, and changes in addictive behaviors. Involved in anxiety and panic disorders.
Insula_L.funFact: The insula helps you feel your heartbeat and may be key to consciousness and the sense of self.

Insula_R.summary: Right hemisphere hub for body awareness and autonomic control.
Insula_R.function: Specializes in awareness of internal body states and autonomic nervous system regulation. Involved in processing pain and temperature. Right insula may be dominant for sympathetic ('fight or flight') responses.
Insula_R.location: Deep within the right lateral sulcus, covered by the frontal and temporal opercula.
Insula_R.clinical: Right insula damage can cause loss of body awareness and autonomic dysregulation. Implicated in chronic pain conditions.
Insula_R.funFact: The right insula is particularly active when you're aware of your own heartbeat.

Caudate_L.summary: Part of the basal ganglia involved in learning, motivation, and movement control.
Caudate_L.function: Critical for habit learning and procedural memory. Involved in goal-directed behavior and reward processing. Part of cortico-striatal loops that modulate motor and cognitive functions.
Caudate_L.location: C-shaped nucleus in the basal ganglia, curving around the lateral ventricle. Forms the striatum together with the putamen.
Caudate_L.clinical: Degeneration seen in Huntington's disease. Abnormalities linked to OCD, ADHD, and Tourette syndrome. Important for addiction circuitry.
Caudate_L.funFact: The caudate lights up when you're in love - it's part of the brain's reward system.

Caudate_R.summary: Right basal ganglia structure for habit formation and cognitive control.
Caudate_R.function: Involved in learning new habits and automating behaviors. Participates in cognitive flexibility and response inhibition. Part of circuits for eye movement control.
Caudate_R.location: Right basal ganglia, C-shaped structure following the lateral ventricle.
Caudate_R.clinical: Atrophy in Huntington's disease causes motor and cognitive symptoms. Involved in various psychiatric conditions.
Caudate_R.funFact: Experienced chess players show caudate activation when recognizing familiar patterns, reflecting automated expertise.

Putamen_L.summary: Basal ganglia nucleus essential for motor control and learning.
Putamen_L.function: Regulates movements and influences various types of learning. Part of the motor circuit that helps initiate and control movements. Involved in learning motor sequences.
Putamen_L.location: Lateral part of the lentiform nucleus in the basal ganglia, lateral to the globus pallidus.
Putamen_L.clinical: Affected in Parkinson's disease, causing movement difficulties. Damage can cause chorea (involuntary jerky movements).
Putamen_L.funFact: Professional musicians show enlarged putamens, reflecting years of motor skill practice.

Putamen_R.summary: Right hemisphere motor nucleus for movement regulation.
Putamen_R.function: Controls movement patterns for the left side of the body. Involved in motor learning and habit formation. Integrates sensory-motor information.
Putamen_R.location: Right basal ganglia, part of the striatum and lentiform nucleus.
Putamen_R.clinical: Degenerates in Parkinson's disease. Lesions can cause movement disorders affecting the left body.
Putamen_R.funFact: The putamen is essential for learning to ride a bike - once you learn, you never forget thanks to this structure.

Frontal_Sup_2_L.summary: Superior frontal region involved in working memory and cognitive control.
Frontal_Sup_2_L.function: Supports working memory, particularly spatial working memory. Involved in self-referential thinking and introspection. Part of the default mode network.
Frontal_Sup_2_L.location: Superior portion of the left frontal lobe, part of the prefrontal cortex.
Frontal_Sup_2_L.clinical: Damage can impair executive functions and working memory. Reduced activity seen in depression.
Frontal_Sup_2_L.funFact: This region is particularly active when you're thinking about yourself and your own thoughts.

Frontal_Sup_2_R.summary: Right superior frontal cortex supporting attention and spatial cognition.
Frontal_Sup_2_R.function: Involved in sustained attention and spatial working memory. Contributes to motor planning and self-awareness. Part of networks supporting cognitive control.
Frontal_Sup_2_R.location: Superior portion of the right frontal lobe, extending along the superior frontal gyrus.
Frontal_Sup_2_R.clinical: Damage associated with attention deficits and impaired spatial cognition. Abnormalities seen in ADHD.
Frontal_Sup_2_R.funFact: Right frontal regions are particularly important for maintaining vigilance over long periods.

Frontal_Mid_2_L.summary: Middle frontal gyrus critical for executive function and attention.
Frontal_Mid_2_L.function: Key area for executive functions including planning, decision-making, and cognitive flexibility. Houses parts of the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC), crucial for working memory.
Frontal_Mid_2_L.location: Middle portion of the left frontal lobe, between the superior and inferior frontal gyri.
Frontal_Mid_2_L.clinical: Damage impairs planning, organization, and multitasking. Target for TMS treatment in depression. Abnormal in ADHD and schizophrenia.
Frontal_Mid_2_L.funFact: This region acts like a mental workspace where you can juggle multiple pieces of information at once.

Frontal_Mid_2_R.summary: Right middle frontal region for attention control and spatial processing.
Frontal_Mid_2_R.function: Involved in spatial attention and the control of eye movements. Contributes to response inhibition and attentional reorienting. Part of the ventral attention network.
Frontal_Mid_2_R.location: Middle portion of the right frontal lobe, part of the lateral prefrontal cortex.
Frontal_Mid_2_R.clinical: Damage can cause spatial neglect and attention deficits. Involved in ADHD pathology.
Frontal_Mid_2_R.funFact: This region helps you stop yourself from doing something you've already started - like not pressing a button at the last moment.

Occipital_Sup_L.summary: Higher-level visual processing area for complex visual analysis.
Occipital_Sup_L.function: Processes complex visual information beyond basic features. Involved in visual attention and awareness. Contributes to recognition of objects and spatial relationships.
Occipital_Sup_L.location: Superior portion of the left occipital lobe, posterior to the parietal lobe.
Occipital_Sup_L.clinical: Damage can cause visual processing deficits and agnosia. May contribute to visual hallucinations in some conditions.
Occipital_Sup_L.funFact: This area helps you understand what you're looking at, not just see it.

Occipital_Sup_R.summary: Right superior visual cortex for spatial vision and attention.
Occipital_Sup_R.function: Processes visual information with emphasis on spatial aspects. Involved in guiding movements based on visual input. Part of the dorsal visual stream.
Occipital_Sup_R.location: Superior portion of the right occipital lobe, at the posterior pole of the brain.
Occipital_Sup_R.clinical: Damage can impair visuospatial processing and motion perception. Associated with Balint's syndrome when bilateral.
Occipital_Sup_R.funFact: This region helps you reach for objects accurately - it calculates where things are in space.

Temporal_Sup_L.summary: Auditory processing and language comprehension center.
Temporal_Sup_L.function: Contains the primary auditory cortex and Wernicke's area for language comprehension. Processes speech sounds and complex auditory information. Involved in understanding spoken language.
Temporal_Sup_L.location: Superior temporal gyrus of the left temporal lobe, contains Heschl's gyrus.
Temporal_Sup_L.clinical: Damage to Wernicke's area causes fluent aphasia - inability to understand or produce meaningful language. Important for auditory processing disorders.
Temporal_Sup_L.funFact: This region can distinguish between thousands of different words in less than a second.

Temporal_Sup_R.summary: Right auditory cortex specializing in music and prosody.
Temporal_Sup_R.function: Processes non-speech sounds, music, and emotional aspects of speech (prosody). Involved in recognizing voices and environmental sounds. Important for understanding tone and melody.
Temporal_Sup_R.location: Superior temporal gyrus of the right temporal lobe, including auditory cortex.
Temporal_Sup_R.clinical: Damage impairs music perception and recognition of emotional tone in speech. Can cause amusia (inability to perceive music).
Temporal_Sup_R.funFact: Musicians often show enhanced activation in this region, especially when listening to their trained instrument.

Cingulate_Mid_L.summary: Part of the cingulate cortex involved in motor control and cognitive processing.
Cingulate_Mid_L.function: Involved in motor control, especially for complex actions. Processes conflict and errors. Part of the salience network that detects important information.
Cingulate_Mid_L.location: Middle portion of the cingulate gyrus, above the corpus callosum.
Cingulate_Mid_L.clinical: Abnormalities linked to schizophrenia and depression. Important for pain processing.
Cingulate_Mid_L.funFact: This region activates when you realize you've made a mistake, even before you're fully aware of it.

Cingulate_Mid_R.summary: Right midcingulate region for action monitoring and motivation.
Cingulate_Mid_R.function: Monitors actions and detects conflicts between competing responses. Involved in pain processing and motivated behavior. Bridges cognitive and motor functions.
Cingulate_Mid_R.location: Middle portion of the right cingulate gyrus, dorsal to the corpus callosum.
Cingulate_Mid_R.clinical: Abnormalities associated with apathy and akinetic mutism. Involved in chronic pain conditions.
Cingulate_Mid_R.funFact: The midcingulate cortex has been called the 'hub of willpower' - it activates when you push through difficulty.

Cerebellum_Crus1_L.summary: Cerebellar region contributing to cognitive and language functions.
Cerebellum_Crus1_L.function: Unlike other cerebellar regions, Crus I is heavily involved in cognitive tasks. Contributes to language processing, working memory, and executive functions. Part of the cognitive cerebellum.
Cerebellum_Crus1_L.location: Lateral posterior cerebellum, part of the cerebellar hemispheres.
Cerebellum_Crus1_L.clinical: Damage can cause cognitive deficits and language problems, not just motor issues. Involved in autism spectrum conditions.
Cerebellum_Crus1_L.funFact: The cerebellum contains more neurons than the rest of the brain combined, and Crus I helps with thinking, not just moving.

Cerebellum_Crus1_R.summary: Right cerebellar region for cognitive processing and timing.
Cerebellum_Crus1_R.function: Involved in cognitive functions traditionally associated with the cerebral cortex. Contributes to attention, working memory, and language. Helps with precise timing of thoughts and actions.
Cerebellum_Crus1_R.location: Lateral aspect of the right posterior cerebellum.
Cerebellum_Crus1_R.clinical: Cerebellar cognitive affective syndrome can result from damage here. Implicated in autism and dyslexia.
Cerebellum_Crus1_R.funFact: The right cerebellum connects primarily with the left cerebral hemisphere, creating a crossed organization.

Thal_VL_L.summary: Thalamic relay nucleus for motor information.
Thal_VL_L.function: Relays motor information from the basal ganglia and cerebellum to the motor cortex. Essential for voluntary movement control. Filters and modulates motor signals.
Thal_VL_L.location: Ventrolateral nucleus of the left thalamus, part of the motor thalamus.
Thal_VL_L.clinical: Deep brain stimulation target for Parkinson's disease and essential tremor. Lesions can cause movement disorders.
Thal_VL_L.funFact: This tiny region is a major crossroads where signals from multiple brain systems converge before reaching the motor cortex.

Thal_VL_R.summary: Right motor thalamus relaying movement commands.
Thal_VL_R.function: Transmits motor information from cerebellum and basal ganglia to right motor cortex. Essential for smooth, coordinated left-sided movements. Integrates multiple motor signals.
Thal_VL_R.location: Ventrolateral nucleus of the right thalamus.
Thal_VL_R.clinical: Target for deep brain stimulation in movement disorders. Lesions cause contralateral motor deficits.
Thal_VL_R.funFact: Surgeons can lesion this area to stop tremors - the effects are often immediate and dramatic.

Angular_L.summary: Multimodal integration hub for language, mathematics, and spatial cognition.
Angular_L.function: Integrates information from multiple senses. Critical for reading, writing, and mathematical reasoning. Involved in semantic processing and understanding metaphors.
Angular_L.location: Posterior part of the inferior parietal lobule, at the junction of temporal, parietal, and occipital lobes.
Angular_L.clinical: Damage causes Gerstmann syndrome: difficulty with writing, calculating, and distinguishing left from right. Involved in dyslexia and dyscalculia.
Angular_L.funFact: This region lights up when you 'get' a joke or understand a metaphor - it's crucial for abstract thinking.

Angular_R.summary: Right angular gyrus for spatial attention and social cognition.
Angular_R.function: Involved in spatial attention, especially reorienting attention to unexpected stimuli. Contributes to theory of mind and understanding others' perspectives. Part of the default mode network.
Angular_R.location: Posterior inferior parietal lobe of the right hemisphere, at the parieto-temporal-occipital junction.
Angular_R.clinical: Damage can cause spatial neglect and impaired social cognition. Involved in out-of-body experiences when stimulated.
Angular_R.funFact: Stimulating this region can make people feel like they're floating outside their own body.

ACC_pre_L.summary: Pregenual anterior cingulate cortex involved in emotion regulation.
ACC_pre_L.function: Regulates emotional responses and connects emotion with cognition. Involved in processing rewards and punishments. Part of the limbic system and salience network.
ACC_pre_L.location: Anterior portion of the cingulate gyrus, just in front of the genu (knee) of the corpus callosum.
ACC_pre_L.clinical: Abnormalities strongly linked to depression and anxiety. Target for deep brain stimulation in treatment-resistant depression.
ACC_pre_L.funFact: Activity in this region can predict whether an antidepressant will work for a particular person.

ACC_pre_R.summary: Right pregenual cingulate for emotional processing and autonomic control.
ACC_pre_R.function: Modulates emotional responses and autonomic nervous system activity. Involved in processing positive emotions and rewards. Helps regulate stress responses.
ACC_pre_R.location: Anterior cingulate cortex of the right hemisphere, near the genu of corpus callosum.
ACC_pre_R.clinical: Reduced activity associated with depression and PTSD. Involved in emotional blunting.
ACC_pre_R.funFact: The pregenual ACC is more active when you feel loved and socially connected.

ACC_sub_L.summary: Subgenual anterior cingulate cortex critical for mood regulation.
ACC_sub_L.function: Key region for regulating mood and emotional responses. Strongly connected to the amygdala and hypothalamus. Modulates autonomic and endocrine responses to emotional stimuli.
ACC_sub_L.location: Below the genu of the corpus callosum, in the medial prefrontal cortex.
ACC_sub_L.clinical: Hyperactive in depression - deep brain stimulation here can relieve treatment-resistant depression. Abnormal in bipolar disorder.
ACC_sub_L.funFact: This small region, area 25, has been called the 'sadness center' of the brain.

ACC_sub_R.summary: Right subgenual cingulate for mood and visceral responses.
ACC_sub_R.function: Regulates mood states and connects emotional processing with bodily responses. Involved in processing sadness and negative emotions. Modulates autonomic function.
ACC_sub_R.location: Subgenual anterior cingulate of the right hemisphere, ventral to corpus callosum.
ACC_sub_R.clinical: Metabolic abnormalities seen in mood disorders. Target for experimental depression treatments.
ACC_sub_R.funFact: The subgenual cingulate connects your emotions to your gut - explaining why sadness can feel physical.

ACC_sup_L.summary: Dorsal anterior cingulate for cognitive control and conflict monitoring.
ACC_sup_L.function: Monitors for conflicts between competing responses and signals need for increased control. Involved in error detection and performance monitoring. Part of the cognitive control network.
ACC_sup_L.location: Dorsal (upper) portion of the anterior cingulate gyrus, above the corpus callosum.
ACC_sup_L.clinical: Abnormalities linked to OCD, schizophrenia, and ADHD. Important for understanding addiction and impulse control disorders.
ACC_sup_L.funFact: This region activates when you feel the 'oops' of making a mistake - even before you know you made one.

ACC_sup_R.summary: Right dorsal cingulate for attention and response selection.
ACC_sup_R.function: Involved in attention allocation and selecting between competing responses. Processes pain and anticipates negative outcomes. Part of the salience network.
ACC_sup_R.location: Dorsal anterior cingulate of the right hemisphere.
ACC_sup_R.clinical: Involved in attention disorders and chronic pain conditions. Abnormal in anxiety disorders.
ACC_sup_R.funFact: The dorsal ACC is sometimes called the brain's 'alarm system' - it alerts you when something needs attention.

Calcarine_L.summary: Primary visual cortex where visual information first arrives.
Calcarine_L.function: Receives direct input from the eyes via the thalamus. Processes basic visual features like edges, orientation, and contrast. Beginning of conscious visual perception.
Calcarine_L.location: Banks of the calcarine sulcus in the medial occipital lobe. Also called V1 or striate cortex.
Calcarine_L.clinical: Damage causes cortical blindness in the opposite visual field. Can cause visual hallucinations (Charles Bonnet syndrome).
Calcarine_L.funFact: The primary visual cortex has a complete map of your visual world - but it's upside down and backwards.

Calcarine_R.summary: Right primary visual cortex processing the left visual field.
Calcarine_R.function: Processes visual information from the left visual field. Detects basic features like lines, edges, and motion. First cortical stage of visual processing.
Calcarine_R.location: Medial surface of the right occipital lobe, surrounding the calcarine fissure.
Calcarine_R.clinical: Damage causes left visual field blindness (homonymous hemianopia). Lesions can cause vivid visual hallucinations.
Calcarine_R.funFact: Even when blind, some people can still sense movement - this 'blindsight' bypasses V1 entirely.

Cerebellum_10_L.summary: Cerebellar region involved in vestibular processing and balance.
Cerebellum_10_L.function: Processes information from the vestibular system for balance and spatial orientation. Involved in coordinating eye movements with head position. Part of the vestibulocerebellum.
Cerebellum_10_L.location: Most inferior part of the left cerebellar hemisphere, near the flocculus.
Cerebellum_10_L.clinical: Damage causes vertigo and balance problems. Important in motion sickness and vestibular disorders.
Cerebellum_10_L.funFact: This region helps you stay balanced even with your eyes closed - it processes signals from your inner ear.

Cerebellum_10_R.summary: Right cerebellar lobule for balance and vestibular integration.
Cerebellum_10_R.function: Integrates vestibular signals for maintaining balance and posture. Coordinates eye movements during head motion. Essential for the vestibulo-ocular reflex.
Cerebellum_10_R.location: Inferior portion of the right cerebellar hemisphere.
Cerebellum_10_R.clinical: Lesions cause imbalance and nystagmus. Involved in vestibular migraine.
Cerebellum_10_R.funFact: Astronauts experience changes in this region during spaceflight as their vestibular system adapts to zero gravity.

Cerebellum_3_L.summary: Anterior cerebellar lobule for leg and trunk motor control.
Cerebellum_3_L.function: Controls movements of the legs and trunk. Receives proprioceptive information from the lower body. Part of the spinocerebellum that regulates posture and gait.
Cerebellum_3_L.location: Anterior lobe of the left cerebellum.
Cerebellum_3_L.clinical: Damage causes gait ataxia and difficulty with leg coordination. Affected early in alcoholic cerebellar degeneration.
Cerebellum_3_L.funFact: This region is heavily connected to your spinal cord, constantly adjusting your posture without conscious effort.

Cerebellum_3_R.summary: Right anterior cerebellum for lower body coordination.
Cerebellum_3_R.function: Coordinates movements of the trunk and lower limbs. Integrates proprioceptive feedback for smooth movements. Important for maintaining posture.
Cerebellum_3_R.location: Anterior lobe of the right cerebellum.
Cerebellum_3_R.clinical: Damage causes ataxic gait and postural instability. Affected in multiple system atrophy.
Cerebellum_3_R.funFact: Chronic alcohol abuse particularly damages this region, explaining the characteristic unsteady gait of alcoholics.

Cerebellum_4_5_L.summary: Cerebellar lobules IV-V for arm and hand coordination.
Cerebellum_4_5_L.function: Controls fine movements of the upper limbs. Integrates sensory feedback for precise hand movements. Important for motor learning and adaptation.
Cerebellum_4_5_L.location: Anterior lobe of the left cerebellum, posterior to lobule III.
Cerebellum_4_5_L.clinical: Damage causes limb ataxia and intention tremor. Affected in spinocerebellar ataxias.
Cerebellum_4_5_L.funFact: This region is essential for activities requiring precision, like threading a needle or playing piano.

Cerebellum_4_5_R.summary: Right cerebellar lobules IV-V for upper limb control.
Cerebellum_4_5_R.function: Coordinates movements of the left arm and hand. Processes proprioceptive information from upper limbs. Involved in motor adaptation and learning.
Cerebellum_4_5_R.location: Anterior lobe of the right cerebellum.
Cerebellum_4_5_R.clinical: Lesions cause left upper limb ataxia. Involved in essential tremor.
Cerebellum_4_5_R.funFact: When you learn a new motor skill, this region shows increased activity until the skill becomes automatic.

Cerebellum_6_L.summary: Cerebellar lobule VI bridging motor and cognitive functions.
Cerebellum_6_L.function: Transition zone between motor and cognitive cerebellar regions. Involved in both movement control and cognitive tasks like language and attention.
Cerebellum_6_L.location: Superior posterior lobe of the left cerebellum, between anterior and posterior lobes.
Cerebellum_6_L.clinical: Damage can cause both motor and cognitive symptoms. Implicated in developmental coordination disorder.
Cerebellum_6_L.funFact: This region activates when you imagine performing a movement without actually moving - it plans actions.

Cerebellum_6_R.summary: Right cerebellar lobule VI for motor-cognitive integration.
Cerebellum_6_R.function: Integrates motor control with cognitive processing. Involved in movement planning and timing. Contributes to language and spatial processing.
Cerebellum_6_R.location: Superior posterior lobe of the right cerebellum.
Cerebellum_6_R.clinical: Affected in cerebellar cognitive affective syndrome. Abnormalities seen in autism.
Cerebellum_6_R.funFact: Professional athletes show enhanced development of this region from years of coordinated practice.

Cerebellum_7b_L.summary: Left cerebellar lobule VIIb for cognitive processing.
Cerebellum_7b_L.function: Involved in higher cognitive functions rather than pure motor control. Contributes to working memory and executive functions. Part of the cognitive cerebellum.
Cerebellum_7b_L.location: Posterior lobe of the left cerebellum.
Cerebellum_7b_L.clinical: Damage can cause cognitive deficits including problems with planning and timing.
Cerebellum_7b_L.funFact: The more posterior cerebellar regions like this are evolutionarily newer and support our unique human cognitive abilities.

Cerebellum_7b_R.summary: Right cerebellar VIIb for cognitive and attentional functions.
Cerebellum_7b_R.function: Contributes to cognitive tasks including attention and working memory. Involved in timing and sequence processing. Connected to prefrontal cortex.
Cerebellum_7b_R.location: Posterior lobe of the right cerebellum.
Cerebellum_7b_R.clinical: Abnormalities linked to cognitive symptoms in cerebellar disorders. Involved in ADHD.
Cerebellum_7b_R.funFact: This region helps you estimate time intervals - damage here can impair your sense of timing.

Cerebellum_8_L.summary: Cerebellar lobule VIII for sensorimotor integration.
Cerebellum_8_L.function: Integrates sensory information with motor commands. Involved in sensorimotor tasks and some cognitive functions. Part of the default mode cerebellar network.
Cerebellum_8_L.location: Inferior posterior lobe of the left cerebellum.
Cerebellum_8_L.clinical: Lesions affect coordination and may impact some cognitive functions.
Cerebellum_8_L.funFact: This region is active when you're mind-wandering, suggesting a role in the brain's default mode.

Cerebellum_8_R.summary: Right cerebellar VIII for somatosensory processing.
Cerebellum_8_R.function: Processes somatosensory information for motor control. Involved in body image and proprioception. Contributes to sensorimotor integration.
Cerebellum_8_R.location: Inferior posterior lobe of the right cerebellum.
Cerebellum_8_R.clinical: Damage affects sensorimotor coordination. Involved in body representation disorders.
Cerebellum_8_R.funFact: This region contains a second body map in the cerebellum, separate from the one in the anterior lobe.

Cerebellum_9_L.summary: Cerebellar lobule IX for default mode and vestibular processing.
Cerebellum_9_L.function: Part of the cerebellar default mode network. Involved in vestibular processing and self-referential thought. Contributes to social cognition.
Cerebellum_9_L.location: Inferior posterior cerebellum, near the uvula.
Cerebellum_9_L.clinical: Abnormalities linked to autism and social cognitive deficits.
Cerebellum_9_L.funFact: This region activates during daydreaming and thinking about yourself - connecting cerebellum to self-awareness.

Cerebellum_9_R.summary: Right cerebellar IX for vestibular and social processing.
Cerebellum_9_R.function: Processes vestibular information and contributes to default mode network function. Involved in social cognition and theory of mind. Part of posterior cerebellum.
Cerebellum_9_R.location: Inferior posterior lobe of the right cerebellum.
Cerebellum_9_R.clinical: Implicated in autism spectrum conditions. Damage can affect social understanding.
Cerebellum_9_R.funFact: Research suggests the cerebellum helps us predict others' actions - essential for smooth social interaction.

Cerebellum_Crus2_L.summary: Left cerebellar Crus II for executive and language functions.
Cerebellum_Crus2_L.function: Heavily involved in executive functions and language processing. Contributes to verbal working memory and semantic processing. Part of the cognitive cerebellum.
Cerebellum_Crus2_L.location: Lateral posterior cerebellum, posterior to Crus I.
Cerebellum_Crus2_L.clinical: Damage can cause executive dysfunction and language problems. Implicated in autism and dyslexia.
Cerebellum_Crus2_L.funFact: This region is more active during verbal tasks than motor tasks - the cerebellum does much more than coordinate movement.

Cerebellum_Crus2_R.summary: Right cerebellar Crus II for cognitive and spatial functions.
Cerebellum_Crus2_R.function: Involved in spatial cognition and executive functions. Contributes to attention and working memory. Connected to prefrontal and parietal cortex.
Cerebellum_Crus2_R.location: Lateral posterior cerebellum of the right hemisphere.
Cerebellum_Crus2_R.clinical: Cerebellar cognitive affective syndrome can result from damage here. Involved in autism.
Cerebellum_Crus2_R.funFact: The right Crus II is particularly involved in spatial working memory - remembering where things are.

Cingulate_Post_L.summary: Posterior cingulate cortex - hub of the default mode network.
Cingulate_Post_L.function: Central node of the default mode network, active during self-reflection and memory retrieval. Involved in autobiographical memory and thinking about the past and future.
Cingulate_Post_L.location: Posterior portion of the cingulate gyrus, behind the central sulcus.
Cingulate_Post_L.clinical: One of the earliest regions affected in Alzheimer's disease. Reduced activity correlates with memory impairment.
Cingulate_Post_L.funFact: The posterior cingulate is most active when you're thinking about nothing in particular - it's your daydreaming center.

Cingulate_Post_R.summary: Right posterior cingulate for memory and self-referential processing.
Cingulate_Post_R.function: Part of the default mode network supporting autobiographical memory and introspection. Involved in spatial memory and navigation. Integrates internal and external information.
Cingulate_Post_R.location: Posterior cingulate gyrus of the right hemisphere.
Cingulate_Post_R.clinical: Early involvement in Alzheimer's disease. Shows reduced metabolism in mild cognitive impairment.
Cingulate_Post_R.funFact: Activity in this region decreases when you're focused on a challenging task - it turns off when you're concentrating.

Cuneus_L.summary: Visual processing area for the lower visual field.
Cuneus_L.function: Processes visual information from the lower part of the visual field. Involved in basic visual processing and visual attention. Part of the dorsal visual stream.
Cuneus_L.location: Medial surface of the occipital lobe, above the calcarine sulcus. Wedge-shaped (cuneus means 'wedge').
Cuneus_L.clinical: Damage can cause lower visual field defects. Involved in some visual processing disorders.
Cuneus_L.funFact: The cuneus processes what you see below eye level - useful for watching your step while walking.

Cuneus_R.summary: Right cuneus for lower visual field processing.
Cuneus_R.function: Processes visual information from the left lower visual field. Involved in visual processing and visual spatial attention. Contributes to visual perception.
Cuneus_R.location: Medial occipital cortex of the right hemisphere, superior to the calcarine fissure.
Cuneus_R.clinical: Lesions cause left lower quadrant visual field loss. Can be involved in migraine aura.
Cuneus_R.funFact: During meditation, the cuneus shows altered activity patterns - visual processing changes when eyes are closed.

Frontal_Inf_Oper_L.summary: Broca's area - essential for speech production and language.
Frontal_Inf_Oper_L.function: Contains Broca's area, critical for speech production and language processing. Involved in phonological processing and verbal working memory. Also involved in action understanding.
Frontal_Inf_Oper_L.location: Posterior part of the inferior frontal gyrus, pars opercularis. Adjacent to motor cortex.
Frontal_Inf_Oper_L.clinical: Damage causes Broca's aphasia - non-fluent speech with intact comprehension. Important for stroke recovery.
Frontal_Inf_Oper_L.funFact: Broca's area contains mirror neurons that fire both when you perform an action and when you see someone else do it.

Frontal_Inf_Oper_R.summary: Right inferior frontal operculum for response inhibition.
Frontal_Inf_Oper_R.function: Critical for response inhibition - stopping yourself from doing something. Involved in attention and processing prosody (emotional tone of speech). Part of the ventral attention network.
Frontal_Inf_Oper_R.location: Posterior inferior frontal gyrus of the right hemisphere.
Frontal_Inf_Oper_R.clinical: Damage impairs ability to stop actions. Abnormal in ADHD and impulse control disorders.
Frontal_Inf_Oper_R.funFact: This region is the brain's 'brake pedal' - it's essential for self-control and resisting temptations.

Frontal_Inf_Orb_2_L.summary: Left orbital inferior frontal cortex for semantic processing.
Frontal_Inf_Orb_2_L.function: Involved in semantic processing and controlled semantic retrieval. Contributes to language comprehension and emotional processing. Part of the language network.
Frontal_Inf_Orb_2_L.location: Orbital part of the left inferior frontal gyrus, above the eye socket.
Frontal_Inf_Orb_2_L.clinical: Damage can cause word-finding difficulties. Involved in semantic dementia.
Frontal_Inf_Orb_2_L.funFact: This region activates when you're trying to think of a word that's 'on the tip of your tongue.'

Frontal_Inf_Orb_2_R.summary: Right orbital frontal cortex for social and emotional processing.
Frontal_Inf_Orb_2_R.function: Involved in social cognition and emotional processing. Contributes to understanding others' intentions and emotions. Part of the social brain network.
Frontal_Inf_Orb_2_R.location: Orbital part of the right inferior frontal gyrus.
Frontal_Inf_Orb_2_R.clinical: Damage can cause inappropriate social behavior. Involved in autism and psychopathy.
Frontal_Inf_Orb_2_R.funFact: This region helps you understand social cues like sarcasm and irony.

Frontal_Inf_Tri_L.summary: Left triangular inferior frontal cortex for semantic processing.
Frontal_Inf_Tri_L.function: Part of Broca's region involved in semantic processing and language production. Important for semantic selection and verbal fluency. Contributes to syntax processing.
Frontal_Inf_Tri_L.location: Triangular part of the left inferior frontal gyrus (pars triangularis).
Frontal_Inf_Tri_L.clinical: Damage contributes to language deficits. Important for semantic aspects of language disorders.
Frontal_Inf_Tri_L.funFact: This region is larger on the left side in most people - one of the brain asymmetries that makes humans unique.

Frontal_Inf_Tri_R.summary: Right triangular frontal cortex for prosody and attention.
Frontal_Inf_Tri_R.function: Involved in processing emotional prosody and attention reorienting. Contributes to understanding non-literal language like metaphors. Part of the right hemisphere language network.
Frontal_Inf_Tri_R.location: Triangular part of the right inferior frontal gyrus.
Frontal_Inf_Tri_R.clinical: Damage can impair understanding of emotional tone in speech. Involved in pragmatic language disorders.
Frontal_Inf_Tri_R.funFact: While the left side handles the words, this right side helps you understand what someone really means.

Frontal_Med_Orb_L.summary: Medial orbitofrontal cortex for reward and value processing.
Frontal_Med_Orb_L.function: Processes reward value and makes value-based decisions. Involved in subjective pleasure and emotional regulation. Part of the brain's reward system.
Frontal_Med_Orb_L.location: Medial portion of the orbitofrontal cortex, above the eye sockets.
Frontal_Med_Orb_L.clinical: Damage causes changes in personality and decision-making. Abnormal in depression and addiction.
Frontal_Med_Orb_L.funFact: This region encodes how much you like something - from food to music to art.

Frontal_Med_Orb_R.summary: Right medial orbital cortex for value judgments.
Frontal_Med_Orb_R.function: Evaluates subjective value of rewards and outcomes. Involved in emotional decision-making and social cognition. Contributes to understanding others' mental states.
Frontal_Med_Orb_R.location: Medial orbitofrontal cortex of the right hemisphere.
Frontal_Med_Orb_R.clinical: Damage associated with poor decision-making and impulsivity. Involved in addiction circuits.
Frontal_Med_Orb_R.funFact: When you regret a decision, this region becomes more active - it tracks what could have been.

Frontal_Sup_Medial_L.summary: Medial prefrontal cortex for self-referential processing.
Frontal_Sup_Medial_L.function: Central to self-referential thinking and mentalizing about others. Part of the default mode network. Involved in social cognition and moral reasoning.
Frontal_Sup_Medial_L.location: Medial surface of the superior frontal gyrus, part of medial prefrontal cortex.
Frontal_Sup_Medial_L.clinical: Abnormalities linked to depression and autism. Important for understanding social cognition disorders.
Frontal_Sup_Medial_L.funFact: This region is active when you think about yourself - it's your neural autobiography writer.

Frontal_Sup_Medial_R.summary: Right medial frontal cortex for social cognition.
Frontal_Sup_Medial_R.function: Involved in theory of mind - understanding others' thoughts and feelings. Part of the default mode network. Contributes to self-awareness and introspection.
Frontal_Sup_Medial_R.location: Medial surface of the right superior frontal gyrus.
Frontal_Sup_Medial_R.clinical: Implicated in autism spectrum conditions. Abnormal activity in schizophrenia.
Frontal_Sup_Medial_R.funFact: When you imagine what someone else is thinking, this region simulates their mental state.

Fusiform_L.summary: Fusiform gyrus essential for face and word recognition.
Fusiform_L.function: Contains the fusiform face area (FFA) for face recognition and visual word form area for reading. Critical for expert visual recognition of any category you're trained to distinguish.
Fusiform_L.location: Inferior temporal cortex, on the bottom surface of the temporal lobe.
Fusiform_L.clinical: Damage can cause prosopagnosia (face blindness) or acquired dyslexia. Abnormal activation in autism.
Fusiform_L.funFact: Car experts use this region to recognize cars the same way most people use it to recognize faces.

Fusiform_R.summary: Right fusiform gyrus dominant for face processing.
Fusiform_R.function: Primary region for face perception and recognition. Responds more strongly to faces than any other visual category. Important for emotional face processing.
Fusiform_R.location: Inferior temporal lobe of the right hemisphere, on the ventral surface.
Fusiform_R.clinical: Damage causes prosopagnosia. Reduced activation in autism. Hyperactivation for own-race faces.
Fusiform_R.funFact: The right fusiform is so specialized for faces that it responds more to an upside-down face than a right-side-up house.

Heschl_L.summary: Primary auditory cortex - where hearing begins in the brain.
Heschl_L.function: First cortical region to process auditory information. Analyzes basic sound features like frequency and intensity. Encodes the building blocks of speech and music.
Heschl_L.location: Heschl's gyrus, on the superior temporal plane within the lateral sulcus.
Heschl_L.clinical: Damage causes cortical deafness. Important for auditory processing disorders and tinnitus.
Heschl_L.funFact: Musicians have larger Heschl's gyri, especially those who started training early in life.

Heschl_R.summary: Right primary auditory cortex for pitch and music.
Heschl_R.function: Processes auditory information with specialization for pitch, timbre, and music. Analyzes spectral features of sounds. Important for music perception.
Heschl_R.location: Heschl's gyrus of the right hemisphere, on the superior temporal plane.
Heschl_R.clinical: Lesions can cause amusia (inability to perceive music). Important for tinnitus.
Heschl_R.funFact: The right auditory cortex is better at detecting small pitch changes - crucial for enjoying music.

LC_L.summary: Locus coeruleus - the brain's noradrenaline factory.
LC_L.function: Primary source of noradrenaline (norepinephrine) in the brain. Regulates arousal, attention, and stress responses. Modulates activity across the entire cortex.
LC_L.location: Small nucleus in the brainstem pons, near the floor of the fourth ventricle. Despite tiny size, projects throughout the brain.
LC_L.clinical: Degenerates early in Alzheimer's and Parkinson's diseases. Target for understanding arousal and attention disorders.
LC_L.funFact: Named 'blue spot' (locus coeruleus) because it contains melanin that gives it a bluish color.

LC_R.summary: Right locus coeruleus for arousal and vigilance.
LC_R.function: Releases norepinephrine to modulate alertness and attention. Responds to novel or important stimuli. Critical for the stress response and fight-or-flight.
LC_R.location: Brainstem pons of the right side, paired structure with left LC.
LC_R.clinical: Early degeneration in neurodegenerative diseases. Involved in PTSD and panic disorder.
LC_R.funFact: The locus coeruleus fires when you're surprised - it's your brain's novelty detector.

Lingual_L.summary: Visual cortex for color and form processing.
Lingual_L.function: Processes visual information, particularly color and complex forms. Involved in visual word recognition and letter identification. Part of the ventral visual stream.
Lingual_L.location: Medial occipitotemporal cortex, below the calcarine sulcus.
Lingual_L.clinical: Damage can cause color perception deficits (cerebral achromatopsia). Important for reading disorders.
Lingual_L.funFact: This region helps you see in color - damage here can make the world appear in shades of gray.

Lingual_R.summary: Right lingual gyrus for visual processing and imagery.
Lingual_R.function: Processes visual information including color and complex patterns. Involved in visual imagery and dreaming. Contributes to scene perception.
Lingual_R.location: Medial occipitotemporal region of the right hemisphere.
Lingual_R.clinical: Lesions can cause visual field defects and color blindness. Active during visual hallucinations.
Lingual_R.funFact: The lingual gyrus is highly active during vivid dreams - it creates the visual content of your dreams.

N_Acc_L.summary: Nucleus accumbens - center of the brain's reward system.
N_Acc_L.function: Key structure for reward processing, motivation, and pleasure. Releases dopamine in response to rewards. Critical for goal-directed behavior and addiction.
N_Acc_L.location: Ventral striatum, where the caudate and putamen meet. Part of the basal ganglia.
N_Acc_L.clinical: Hyperactivity drives addiction. Target for deep brain stimulation in treatment-resistant depression and OCD.
N_Acc_L.funFact: All addictive drugs, from caffeine to cocaine, increase dopamine release in the nucleus accumbens.

N_Acc_R.summary: Right nucleus accumbens for reward and motivation.
N_Acc_R.function: Processes rewards and motivates goal-directed behavior. Involved in anticipation of pleasure. Central to the brain's reward circuitry.
N_Acc_R.location: Ventral striatum of the right hemisphere.
N_Acc_R.clinical: Dysfunction in addiction and depression. Target for addiction research and treatment.
N_Acc_R.funFact: This region lights up not just when you get a reward, but when you're anticipating one - expectation feels good.

OFCant_L.summary: Anterior orbitofrontal cortex for complex reward evaluation.
OFCant_L.function: Evaluates complex, abstract rewards and outcomes. Involved in social decision-making and moral judgments. Part of the brain's value system.
OFCant_L.location: Anterior portion of the orbitofrontal cortex, behind the forehead.
OFCant_L.clinical: Damage affects social behavior and decision-making. Implicated in addiction and compulsive behaviors.
OFCant_L.funFact: This region helps you value abstract rewards like social approval and long-term goals over immediate pleasures.

OFCant_R.summary: Right anterior orbital cortex for reward and social behavior.
OFCant_R.function: Processes reward value in social contexts. Involved in evaluating social rewards and punishments. Contributes to appropriate social behavior.
OFCant_R.location: Anterior orbitofrontal cortex of the right hemisphere.
OFCant_R.clinical: Damage causes socially inappropriate behavior. Abnormal in psychopathy and addiction.
OFCant_R.funFact: People with damage here may laugh at inappropriate moments or make embarrassing social comments without realizing it.

OFClat_L.summary: Lateral orbitofrontal cortex for punishment and behavioral adjustment.
OFClat_L.function: Processes negative outcomes and punishments. Involved in changing behavior when circumstances change. Important for reversal learning.
OFClat_L.location: Lateral portion of the orbitofrontal cortex.
OFClat_L.clinical: Abnormalities linked to impulsivity and addiction. Important for understanding OCD.
OFClat_L.funFact: This region helps you learn from mistakes - it updates your behavior when something doesn't work out.

OFClat_R.summary: Right lateral orbital cortex for behavioral control.
OFClat_R.function: Monitors negative outcomes and adjusts behavior accordingly. Involved in response inhibition and behavioral flexibility. Processes social punishments.
OFClat_R.location: Lateral orbitofrontal cortex of the right hemisphere.
OFClat_R.clinical: Dysfunction in OCD and impulse control disorders. Important for addiction.
OFClat_R.funFact: When you feel embarrassed about a social mistake, this region is helping you learn not to repeat it.

OFCmed_L.summary: Medial orbitofrontal cortex for subjective value and emotion.
OFCmed_L.function: Encodes subjective value of rewards and emotional stimuli. Involved in positive emotions and reward-related decision making. Part of the limbic system.
OFCmed_L.location: Medial portion of the orbitofrontal cortex, near the midline.
OFCmed_L.clinical: Reduced activity in depression. Damage causes emotional blunting and poor decision-making.
OFCmed_L.funFact: Activity in this region correlates with how much you enjoy something - it's your pleasure meter.

OFCmed_R.summary: Right medial orbital cortex for value and emotional processing.
OFCmed_R.function: Processes subjective value of experiences and outcomes. Involved in hedonic (pleasure) experiences. Integrates emotion with decision-making.
OFCmed_R.location: Medial orbitofrontal cortex of the right hemisphere.
OFCmed_R.clinical: Abnormal in depression and anhedonia. Important for understanding reward-related disorders.
OFCmed_R.funFact: When you savor a delicious meal, this region is encoding exactly how pleasurable it is.

OFCpost_L.summary: Posterior orbitofrontal cortex for sensory-emotion integration.
OFCpost_L.function: Integrates sensory information with emotional value. Processes taste and smell rewards. Involved in evaluating the pleasantness of sensory experiences.
OFCpost_L.location: Posterior portion of the orbitofrontal cortex, closer to primary sensory areas.
OFCpost_L.clinical: Damage affects appreciation of food and other sensory pleasures. Involved in eating disorders.
OFCpost_L.funFact: This region decides whether food tastes good - it's why the same food tastes better when you're hungry.

OFCpost_R.summary: Right posterior orbital cortex for sensory reward processing.
OFCpost_R.function: Evaluates pleasantness of sensory stimuli including taste and smell. Integrates visceral sensations with reward value. Important for food preferences.
OFCpost_R.location: Posterior orbitofrontal cortex of the right hemisphere.
OFCpost_R.clinical: Altered in eating disorders and obesity. Involved in sensory processing abnormalities.
OFCpost_R.funFact: Restaurants know that pleasant smells activate this region, making food seem more delicious.

Occipital_Inf_L.summary: Inferior occipital cortex for object recognition.
Occipital_Inf_L.function: Processes visual information for object recognition. Part of the ventral visual stream. Involved in identifying visual stimuli before they reach higher areas.
Occipital_Inf_L.location: Inferior portion of the occipital lobe, on the lateral surface.
Occipital_Inf_L.clinical: Damage can cause visual agnosia - inability to recognize objects despite intact vision.
Occipital_Inf_L.funFact: This region processes the visual features of objects that allow you to recognize them later.

Occipital_Inf_R.summary: Right inferior occipital cortex for visual processing.
Occipital_Inf_R.function: Early stage of the ventral visual stream for object and face processing. Processes complex visual features. Provides input to fusiform face area.
Occipital_Inf_R.location: Inferior occipital lobe of the right hemisphere.
Occipital_Inf_R.clinical: Lesions contribute to prosopagnosia when combined with fusiform damage. Important for visual recognition.
Occipital_Inf_R.funFact: Visual information passes through here on its way to the 'what' pathway that identifies objects.

Occipital_Mid_L.summary: Middle occipital cortex for intermediate visual processing.
Occipital_Mid_L.function: Intermediate visual processing area. Involved in motion perception and visual attention. Part of both dorsal and ventral visual streams.
Occipital_Mid_L.location: Middle portion of the occipital lobe, lateral occipital complex.
Occipital_Mid_L.clinical: Damage can cause motion blindness (akinetopsia). Important for visual processing disorders.
Occipital_Mid_L.funFact: This region contains area MT/V5, which is specialized for seeing motion - cars, people, balls in flight.

Occipital_Mid_R.summary: Right middle occipital cortex for motion and spatial vision.
Occipital_Mid_R.function: Processes visual motion and spatial information. Involved in tracking moving objects and visual attention. Contains motion-sensitive area MT/V5.
Occipital_Mid_R.location: Middle occipital lobe of the right hemisphere.
Occipital_Mid_R.clinical: Lesions can cause motion blindness. Important for visually guided movements.
Occipital_Mid_R.funFact: Damage here can make the world appear as a series of still images, like watching a slideshow instead of a movie.

Olfactory_L.function: Primary region for processing smell information. Unique among senses in connecting directly to limbic system without thalamic relay. Involved in odor identification and memory.
Olfactory_L.location: Olfactory sulcus and anterior perforated substance, ventral frontal lobe.
Olfactory_L.clinical: Loss of smell (anosmia) can be early sign of Parkinson's and Alzheimer's. Important for COVID-19 related smell loss.
Olfactory_L.funFact: Smell is the only sense with a direct connection to memory centers - that's why scents trigger vivid memories.

Olfactory_R.summary: Right olfactory cortex for odor processing.
Olfactory_R.function: Processes odor information and connects to emotional and memory systems. Involved in odor identification and hedonic (pleasant/unpleasant) judgments.
Olfactory_R.location: Ventral surface of the right frontal lobe, near the orbital cortex.
Olfactory_R.clinical: Smell dysfunction is an early marker of neurodegeneration. Important for quality of life.
Olfactory_R.funFact: Humans can distinguish over a trillion different odors - more than we have words for colors.

Pallidum_L.summary: Globus pallidus - output station of the basal ganglia.
Pallidum_L.function: Main output nucleus of the basal ganglia. Regulates motor activity by inhibiting the thalamus. Critical for movement initiation and termination.
Pallidum_L.location: Medial part of the lentiform nucleus, deep in the brain.
Pallidum_L.clinical: Target for deep brain stimulation in Parkinson's disease and dystonia. Lesions cause movement disorders.
Pallidum_L.funFact: The pallidum keeps your muscles from moving until you want them to - it's constantly applying the brakes.

Pallidum_R.summary: Right globus pallidus for motor control.
Pallidum_R.function: Output relay of right basal ganglia controlling left body movements. Tonically inhibits thalamus to prevent unwanted movements. Releases inhibition to allow intended movements.
Pallidum_R.location: Medial lentiform nucleus of the right hemisphere.
Pallidum_R.clinical: Deep brain stimulation target for movement disorders. Lesions cause contralateral motor symptoms.
Pallidum_R.funFact: In Parkinson's disease, the pallidum becomes overactive, which is why DBS here can relieve symptoms.

ParaHippocampal_L.summary: Parahippocampal gyrus for memory encoding and scene processing.
ParaHippocampal_L.function: Critical for encoding new memories and recognizing places. Contains the parahippocampal place area (PPA) for scene recognition. Gateway to the hippocampus.
ParaHippocampal_L.location: Medial temporal lobe, surrounding the hippocampus.
ParaHippocampal_L.clinical: Damage impairs memory formation and place recognition. Early involvement in Alzheimer's disease.
ParaHippocampal_L.funFact: This region activates strongly when you see pictures of places - it's your brain's GPS system.

ParaHippocampal_R.summary: Right parahippocampal region for spatial memory and scenes.
ParaHippocampal_R.function: Involved in spatial memory and scene recognition. Processes contextual information for memories. Important for navigation and landmark recognition.
ParaHippocampal_R.location: Medial temporal lobe of the right hemisphere, adjacent to the hippocampus.
ParaHippocampal_R.clinical: Damage causes difficulty recognizing familiar places. Important for topographical disorientation.
ParaHippocampal_R.funFact: London taxi drivers have enlarged parahippocampal regions from memorizing the city's complex street layout.

Paracentral_Lobule_L.summary: Paracentral lobule for leg motor and sensory functions.
Paracentral_Lobule_L.function: Contains motor and sensory representations of the leg and foot. Controls voluntary leg movements and processes leg sensations. Involved in bladder control.
Paracentral_Lobule_L.location: Medial surface of frontal and parietal lobes, around the central sulcus.
Paracentral_Lobule_L.clinical: Damage causes contralateral leg weakness and sensory loss. Can cause urinary incontinence.
Paracentral_Lobule_L.funFact: The leg representation is tucked into this fold on the brain's medial surface, hidden from the outside view.

Paracentral_Lobule_R.summary: Right paracentral lobule for left leg function.
Paracentral_Lobule_R.function: Controls left leg movements and processes left leg sensations. Involved in lower limb coordination and bladder control. Contains leg areas of motor and sensory cortex.
Paracentral_Lobule_R.location: Medial surface of the right hemisphere, straddling the central sulcus.
Paracentral_Lobule_R.clinical: Lesions cause left leg weakness and numbness. Bilateral damage can cause incontinence.
Paracentral_Lobule_R.funFact: Both legs' representations are neighbors across the brain's midline, explaining why bilateral injuries are common.

Parietal_Inf_L.summary: Left inferior parietal lobule for tool use and calculation.
Parietal_Inf_L.function: Involved in skilled hand movements, tool use, and mathematical reasoning. Important for reading and writing. Part of the mirror neuron system.
Parietal_Inf_L.location: Lower part of the left parietal lobe, including the supramarginal and angular gyri.
Parietal_Inf_L.clinical: Damage causes apraxia (inability to perform skilled movements) and acalculia. Important for dyslexia.
Parietal_Inf_L.funFact: This region is significantly expanded in humans compared to other primates - it's key to our unique abilities.

Parietal_Inf_R.summary: Right inferior parietal lobule for spatial attention.
Parietal_Inf_R.function: Critical for spatial attention and awareness. Involved in body image and sense of agency. Part of the ventral attention network.
Parietal_Inf_R.location: Lower parietal lobe of the right hemisphere.
Parietal_Inf_R.clinical: Damage causes hemispatial neglect - ignoring the left side of space. Involved in alien hand syndrome.
Parietal_Inf_R.funFact: Damage here can make you completely unaware of half the world - patients may not eat food on one side of their plate.

Parietal_Sup_L.summary: Superior parietal lobule for attention and spatial processing.
Parietal_Sup_L.function: Involved in spatial attention and visuomotor coordination. Important for reaching and grasping movements. Part of the dorsal attention network.
Parietal_Sup_L.location: Upper portion of the left parietal lobe, behind the postcentral gyrus.
Parietal_Sup_L.clinical: Damage causes optic ataxia - inability to reach accurately for objects. Important for visuomotor disorders.
Parietal_Sup_L.funFact: This region calculates how to move your hand to grasp objects - it's doing complex geometry constantly.

Parietal_Sup_R.summary: Right superior parietal cortex for spatial cognition.
Parietal_Sup_R.function: Dominant for spatial attention and processing. Involved in mental rotation and spatial working memory. Controls spatial aspects of attention.
Parietal_Sup_R.location: Superior parietal lobe of the right hemisphere.
Parietal_Sup_R.clinical: Damage causes severe spatial attention deficits. Important for understanding neglect syndrome.
Parietal_Sup_R.funFact: When you rotate an object in your mind, this region is doing the mental heavy lifting.

Postcentral_L.summary: Primary somatosensory cortex for touch sensation from the right body.
Postcentral_L.function: Processes touch, pressure, temperature, and pain from the right side of the body. Contains the sensory homunculus with distorted body map. First cortical stage of tactile perception.
Postcentral_L.location: Postcentral gyrus, just behind the central sulcus in the parietal lobe.
Postcentral_L.clinical: Damage causes numbness and loss of fine touch discrimination on the right side. Important for sensory recovery after stroke.
Postcentral_L.funFact: Your lips and fingers have huge representations here because they're so sensitive - larger than your whole back.

Postcentral_R.summary: Right somatosensory cortex for left body sensation.
Postcentral_R.function: Receives and processes tactile information from the left body. Contains detailed map of left body surface. Discriminates touch qualities like texture and shape.
Postcentral_R.location: Postcentral gyrus of the right parietal lobe.
Postcentral_R.clinical: Lesions cause left-sided sensory loss. Important for understanding somatosensory processing.
Postcentral_R.funFact: The sensory map here is so precise that stimulating specific points creates sensations in corresponding body parts.

Precuneus_L.summary: Precuneus - hub for self-awareness and memory retrieval.
Precuneus_L.function: Key node of the default mode network. Involved in self-reflection, episodic memory retrieval, and visual-spatial imagery. Highly active during rest.
Precuneus_L.location: Medial surface of the parietal lobe, anterior to the occipital lobe.
Precuneus_L.clinical: Reduced metabolism early sign of Alzheimer's. Involved in disorders of consciousness.
Precuneus_L.funFact: The precuneus has one of the highest metabolic rates in the brain - it's always busy, even at rest.

Precuneus_R.summary: Right precuneus for self-awareness and imagery.
Precuneus_R.function: Central to the default mode network. Involved in self-referential processing, mental imagery, and episodic memory. Active during mind-wandering.
Precuneus_R.location: Medial parietal cortex of the right hemisphere.
Precuneus_R.clinical: Deactivation associated with loss of consciousness. Early changes in Alzheimer's disease.
Precuneus_R.funFact: When you visualize something in your mind's eye, the precuneus is creating that mental image.

Raphe_D.summary: Dorsal raphe nucleus - the brain's serotonin factory.
Raphe_D.function: Primary source of serotonin in the brain. Regulates mood, sleep, appetite, and arousal. Projects throughout the entire forebrain.
Raphe_D.location: Brainstem, in the midline of the midbrain and pons.
Raphe_D.clinical: Target of antidepressants (SSRIs). Abnormalities linked to depression, anxiety, and sleep disorders.
Raphe_D.funFact: Despite being tiny, the dorsal raphe sends serotonin to almost every part of the brain.

Raphe_M.summary: Median raphe nucleus for serotonin and behavioral state.
Raphe_M.function: Produces serotonin and regulates behavioral states. Involved in sleep-wake cycles and theta rhythm generation. Projects primarily to hippocampus and limbic structures.
Raphe_M.clinical: Implicated in depression and anxiety. Important for understanding SSRI mechanisms.
Raphe_M.funFact: The raphe nuclei help generate the theta rhythm - the brain wave associated with memory formation and REM sleep.

Rectus_L.summary: Gyrus rectus for social cognition and personality.
Rectus_L.function: Involved in social cognition, personality, and emotional regulation. Part of the medial orbitofrontal cortex. Contributes to self-awareness and social behavior.
Rectus_L.location: Straight gyrus on the ventromedial surface of the frontal lobe, next to the midline.
Rectus_L.clinical: Damage associated with personality changes and poor social judgment. Famous case of Phineas Gage involved this area.
Rectus_L.funFact: This region helps make you 'you' - damage can fundamentally change personality while leaving intellect intact.

Rectus_R.summary: Right gyrus rectus for social and emotional processing.
Rectus_R.function: Processes social and emotional information. Involved in self-referential processing and social decision-making. Part of the ventromedial prefrontal cortex.
Rectus_R.location: Ventromedial frontal lobe of the right hemisphere.
Rectus_R.clinical: Damage affects social behavior and emotional regulation. Important for understanding sociopathy.
Rectus_R.funFact: The size of this region correlates with the size of your social network - bigger region, more friends.

Red_N_L.function: Involved in motor coordination, particularly for arm movements. Part of the rubrospinal tract. Important for motor learning and adaptation.
Red_N_L.location: Midbrain, appears pinkish-red due to high iron content and blood supply.
Red_N_L.clinical: Lesions can cause tremor and motor incoordination. Important for understanding motor system disorders.
Red_N_L.funFact: Named for its pink color, the red nucleus is one of the few brain regions visible to the naked eye in fresh tissue.

Red_N_R.summary: Right red nucleus for left upper limb control.
Red_N_R.function: Coordinates movements of the left upper limb. Receives cerebellar input and sends motor commands. Involved in motor learning.
Red_N_R.location: Right midbrain, paired structure with left red nucleus.
Red_N_R.clinical: Lesions cause contralateral motor symptoms. Can cause rubral tremor.
Red_N_R.funFact: The red nucleus becomes less important in adults but is crucial for motor development in infants.

Rolandic_Oper_L.summary: Rolandic operculum for sensorimotor integration of speech.
Rolandic_Oper_L.function: Integrates sensory and motor information for speech production. Involved in orofacial movements and swallowing. Part of the speech motor network.
Rolandic_Oper_L.location: Frontal and parietal operculum, around the central sulcus (Rolandic fissure).
Rolandic_Oper_L.clinical: Damage can cause speech articulation problems. Important for swallowing disorders (dysphagia).
Rolandic_Oper_L.funFact: This region coordinates over 100 muscles needed for speech - the most complex motor skill humans have.

Rolandic_Oper_R.summary: Right rolandic operculum for face and throat control.
Rolandic_Oper_R.function: Controls movements of the face, tongue, and throat on the left side. Involved in emotional expression and non-verbal vocalizations.
Rolandic_Oper_R.location: Opercular cortex surrounding the right central sulcus.
Rolandic_Oper_R.clinical: Lesions affect facial expression and swallowing. Important for emotional prosody.
Rolandic_Oper_R.funFact: This region is particularly important for expressing emotion through facial movements and voice tone.

SN_pc_L.summary: Substantia nigra pars compacta - dopamine center for movement.
SN_pc_L.function: Produces dopamine essential for movement initiation. Part of the basal ganglia circuit. Loss of neurons here causes Parkinson's disease.
SN_pc_L.location: Midbrain, darkly pigmented due to neuromelanin (hence 'black substance').
SN_pc_L.clinical: Death of dopamine neurons here causes Parkinson's disease. Target for experimental treatments and deep brain stimulation nearby.
SN_pc_L.funFact: By the time Parkinson's symptoms appear, about 60-80% of these dopamine neurons are already lost.

SN_pc_R.summary: Right substantia nigra pars compacta for dopamine production.
SN_pc_R.function: Supplies dopamine to the right striatum for motor control. Essential for initiating and coordinating movements. Loss causes contralateral parkinsonian symptoms.
SN_pc_R.location: Right midbrain, part of the substantia nigra.
SN_pc_R.clinical: Degeneration causes Parkinson's disease. Target of dopamine replacement therapy.
SN_pc_R.funFact: The dark pigment in this region accumulates over life - it's darker in older brains.

SN_pr_L.summary: Substantia nigra pars reticulata - output station of basal ganglia.
SN_pr_L.function: Major output nucleus of the basal ganglia. Controls thalamic activity through inhibition. Important for eye movements and motor function.
SN_pr_L.location: Midbrain, ventral to the pars compacta.
SN_pr_L.clinical: Dysfunction contributes to movement disorders. Important for understanding basal ganglia circuitry.
SN_pr_L.funFact: Unlike pars compacta, this region uses GABA rather than dopamine - it's the inhibitory output.

SN_pr_R.summary: Right substantia nigra pars reticulata for motor output.
SN_pr_R.function: Provides inhibitory output from right basal ganglia. Controls thalamic activity and eye movements. Works with pallidum to regulate motor function.
SN_pr_R.location: Midbrain, ventral portion of the right substantia nigra.
SN_pr_R.clinical: Involved in movement disorders and eye movement abnormalities.
SN_pr_R.funFact: This region helps control rapid eye movements - it releases the 'brake' to allow your eyes to jump to new targets.

Supp_Motor_Area_L.summary: Supplementary motor area for movement planning and sequencing.
Supp_Motor_Area_L.function: Plans and sequences complex movements. Active before voluntary movement begins. Involved in bimanual coordination and learning motor sequences.
Supp_Motor_Area_L.location: Medial surface of the frontal lobe, anterior to the leg area of motor cortex.
Supp_Motor_Area_L.clinical: Damage causes difficulty initiating movements and sequencing actions. SMA syndrome causes temporary mutism.
Supp_Motor_Area_L.funFact: Activity in SMA begins up to a second before you actually move - your brain plans ahead.

Supp_Motor_Area_R.summary: Right SMA for movement initiation and planning.
Supp_Motor_Area_R.function: Plans voluntary movements for the left body. Involved in internally generated movements and motor learning. Coordinates with left SMA for bimanual tasks.
Supp_Motor_Area_R.location: Medial frontal cortex of the right hemisphere.
Supp_Motor_Area_R.clinical: Lesions can cause akinesia (difficulty initiating movement). Important for motor planning disorders.
Supp_Motor_Area_R.funFact: Imagining a movement activates SMA almost as much as actually performing it - basis for motor imagery training.

SupraMarginal_L.summary: Supramarginal gyrus for phonological processing and empathy.
SupraMarginal_L.function: Involved in phonological processing and reading. Important for distinguishing speech sounds. Also involved in empathy and perspective-taking.
SupraMarginal_L.location: Anterior part of the inferior parietal lobule, surrounding the end of the lateral fissure.
SupraMarginal_L.clinical: Damage causes phonological deficits and conduction aphasia. Important for dyslexia research.
SupraMarginal_L.funFact: When you sound out a word while reading, this region is converting letters to sounds.

SupraMarginal_R.summary: Right supramarginal gyrus for spatial processing and empathy.
SupraMarginal_R.function: Involved in spatial attention and body awareness. Important for distinguishing self from others. Contributes to empathy by helping overcome egocentric perspective.
SupraMarginal_R.location: Anterior inferior parietal lobule of the right hemisphere.
SupraMarginal_R.clinical: Damage can cause spatial neglect and body image disturbances. Involved in autism.
SupraMarginal_R.funFact: When you try to see things from someone else's perspective, this region helps you step outside your own viewpoint.

Temporal_Inf_L.summary: Inferior temporal cortex for object recognition.
Temporal_Inf_L.function: High-level visual processing for object recognition. Stores visual representations of objects. Part of the 'what' pathway for identifying things.
Temporal_Inf_L.location: Inferior surface and lateral aspect of the temporal lobe.
Temporal_Inf_L.clinical: Damage causes visual agnosia - inability to recognize objects. Important for semantic dementia.
Temporal_Inf_L.funFact: Different parts of this region represent different categories of objects - tools, animals, faces all have their spots.

Temporal_Inf_R.summary: Right inferior temporal cortex for visual object processing.
Temporal_Inf_R.function: Processes complex visual information for object recognition. Stores visual knowledge about objects. Important for recognizing faces and scenes.
Temporal_Inf_R.location: Inferior temporal lobe of the right hemisphere.
Temporal_Inf_R.clinical: Damage impairs object recognition and visual memory. Can cause category-specific agnosia.
Temporal_Inf_R.funFact: Neurons here can be so specific that single cells respond to pictures of particular celebrities or landmarks.

Temporal_Mid_L.summary: Middle temporal gyrus for semantic processing and language.
Temporal_Mid_L.function: Involved in semantic processing - understanding the meaning of words and concepts. Important for reading comprehension and word retrieval. Part of the language network.
Temporal_Mid_L.location: Middle temporal gyrus of the left temporal lobe.
Temporal_Mid_L.clinical: Damage causes word-finding difficulties and semantic deficits. Affected in semantic dementia.
Temporal_Mid_L.funFact: This region stores your mental dictionary - all the word meanings you've learned throughout life.

Temporal_Mid_R.summary: Right middle temporal gyrus for visual and semantic processing.
Temporal_Mid_R.function: Involved in processing biological motion and social perception. Contributes to face processing and emotional understanding. Part of the social brain network.
Temporal_Mid_R.location: Middle temporal gyrus of the right temporal lobe.
Temporal_Mid_R.clinical: Damage can impair social perception and motion processing. Important for autism research.
Temporal_Mid_R.funFact: This region is exquisitely sensitive to biological motion - you can recognize a walking person from just a few dots.

Temporal_Pole_Mid_L.summary: Left middle temporal pole for semantic memory.
Temporal_Pole_Mid_L.function: Stores general semantic knowledge about the world. Important for conceptual knowledge and naming. Acts as a semantic hub connecting various types of information.
Temporal_Pole_Mid_L.location: Anterior portion of the temporal lobe, at the temporal pole.
Temporal_Pole_Mid_L.clinical: Degenerates in semantic dementia, causing loss of word meanings. Important for naming disorders.
Temporal_Pole_Mid_L.funFact: This region links all your knowledge about concepts - it knows that a dog barks, has fur, and is an animal.

Temporal_Pole_Mid_R.summary: Right middle temporal pole for social concepts.
Temporal_Pole_Mid_R.function: Involved in social and emotional conceptual knowledge. Processes knowledge about people and social situations. Important for understanding social context.
Temporal_Pole_Mid_R.location: Middle portion of the right temporal pole.
Temporal_Pole_Mid_R.clinical: Affected in behavioral variant frontotemporal dementia. Important for social cognition disorders.
Temporal_Pole_Mid_R.funFact: This region stores your social knowledge - what you know about different types of people and social situations.

Temporal_Pole_Sup_L.summary: Left superior temporal pole for social-emotional processing.
Temporal_Pole_Sup_L.function: Integrates emotional and social information. Involved in autobiographical memory and self-reference. Connects emotional processing with language.
Temporal_Pole_Sup_L.location: Superior aspect of the left temporal pole.
Temporal_Pole_Sup_L.clinical: Abnormalities in autism and schizophrenia. Important for emotional aspects of language.
Temporal_Pole_Sup_L.funFact: This region helps you understand the emotional connotations of words - why 'murder' feels different from 'killing.'

Temporal_Pole_Sup_R.summary: Right superior temporal pole for social cognition.
Temporal_Pole_Sup_R.function: Critical for social cognition and theory of mind. Processes emotional aspects of faces and voices. Important for understanding others' mental states.
Temporal_Pole_Sup_R.location: Anterior superior temporal lobe of the right hemisphere.
Temporal_Pole_Sup_R.clinical: Implicated in autism and psychopathy. Damage affects social understanding.
Temporal_Pole_Sup_R.funFact: When you watch a movie and feel what a character feels, this region is helping you empathize.

Thal_AV_L.summary: Anterior ventral thalamus for memory and emotion circuits.
Thal_AV_L.function: Part of the Papez circuit for memory and emotion. Relays information between hippocampus and cingulate cortex. Important for memory consolidation.
Thal_AV_L.location: Anterior portion of the thalamus, near the midline.
Thal_AV_L.clinical: Damage causes memory problems. Involved in Korsakoff syndrome from alcohol abuse.
Thal_AV_L.funFact: This small nucleus is a critical relay station in the circuit that converts experiences into lasting memories.

Thal_AV_R.summary: Right anterior ventral thalamus for limbic connections.
Thal_AV_R.function: Connects limbic structures for memory and emotional processing. Part of the episodic memory system. Relays between hippocampus and cortex.
Thal_AV_R.location: Anterior thalamus of the right hemisphere.
Thal_AV_R.clinical: Lesions cause anterograde amnesia. Important for understanding memory disorders.
Thal_AV_R.funFact: The anterior thalamus is highly active during REM sleep - it may help consolidate memories during dreams.

Thal_IL_L.summary: Intralaminar thalamic nuclei for arousal and awareness.
Thal_IL_L.function: Regulates arousal, attention, and consciousness. Broadcasts signals widely to cortex. Part of the ascending reticular activating system.
Thal_IL_L.location: Within the internal medullary lamina of the thalamus.
Thal_IL_L.clinical: Damage causes severe impairment of consciousness. Target for deep brain stimulation in disorders of consciousness.
Thal_IL_L.funFact: These tiny nuclei help keep you conscious - damage to both sides can cause coma.

Thal_IL_R.summary: Right intralaminar thalamus for consciousness and attention.
Thal_IL_R.function: Maintains cortical arousal and awareness. Involved in attention and motor control via striatal connections. Important for sleep-wake regulation.
Thal_IL_R.location: Internal medullary lamina of the right thalamus.
Thal_IL_R.clinical: Involved in disorders of consciousness. Potential DBS target for minimally conscious state.
Thal_IL_R.funFact: Stimulating these nuclei has helped some patients in minimally conscious state regain awareness.

Thal_LGN_L.summary: Lateral geniculate nucleus - gateway for visual information.
Thal_LGN_L.function: Relays visual information from the retina to the visual cortex. Processes and filters visual signals. First processing stage for conscious vision.
