Thal_LGN_L.location: Lateral and posterior thalamus, part of the metathalamus.
Thal_LGN_L.clinical: Damage causes blindness in the contralateral visual field. Important for visual processing disorders.
Thal_LGN_L.funFact: The LGN doesn't just relay - it actively processes visual information, separating color from motion from form.

Thal_LGN_R.summary: Right lateral geniculate nucleus for left visual field.
Thal_LGN_R.function: Transmits visual information from the left visual field to right visual cortex. Organizes visual input into layers for different aspects of vision.
Thal_LGN_R.location: Lateral posterior thalamus of the right hemisphere.
Thal_LGN_R.clinical: Lesions cause left-sided blindness. Important for visual field defects.
Thal_LGN_R.funFact: The LGN has six distinct layers, each processing different aspects of vision like color and motion.

Thal_LP_L.summary: Lateral posterior thalamus for visual attention.
Thal_LP_L.function: Involved in visual attention and spatial processing. Connects with parietal cortex for visuospatial functions. Part of the pulvinar complex.
Thal_LP_L.clinical: Damage can cause visuospatial attention deficits. Important for understanding visual neglect.
Thal_LP_L.funFact: This region helps you pay attention to relevant visual information while ignoring distractions.

Thal_LP_R.summary: Right lateral posterior thalamus for spatial attention.
Thal_LP_R.function: Critical for visuospatial attention. Connects occipital and parietal cortices. Involved in shifting attention to visual stimuli.
Thal_LP_R.location: Posterior-lateral aspect of the right thalamus.
Thal_LP_R.clinical: Lesions contribute to visual attention deficits. Important for spatial neglect.
Thal_LP_R.funFact: The pulvinar region is massively expanded in humans compared to other primates, suggesting roles in uniquely human cognition.

Thal_MDl_L.summary: Mediodorsal thalamus lateral part for executive function.
Thal_MDl_L.function: Connects with prefrontal cortex for executive functions. Involved in working memory, planning, and decision-making. Part of frontal-subcortical circuits.
Thal_MDl_L.clinical: Damage causes executive dysfunction and memory problems. Affected in schizophrenia.
Thal_MDl_L.funFact: This thalamic nucleus is essentially the frontal lobe's main partner in the thalamus.

Thal_MDl_R.summary: Right mediodorsal lateral thalamus for cognition.
Thal_MDl_R.function: Supports executive functions through prefrontal connections. Involved in cognitive flexibility and working memory.
Thal_MDl_R.location: Lateral mediodorsal thalamus of the right hemisphere.
Thal_MDl_R.clinical: Abnormalities in schizophrenia. Damage affects executive functions.
Thal_MDl_R.funFact: The MD thalamus grows proportionally with the prefrontal cortex across evolution - bigger frontal lobe, bigger MD.

Thal_MDm_L.summary: Mediodorsal medial thalamus for emotion and memory.
Thal_MDm_L.function: Connects with orbitofrontal and medial prefrontal cortex. Involved in emotional processing and memory. Part of the limbic thalamus.
Thal_MDm_L.location: Medial part of the mediodorsal nucleus.
Thal_MDm_L.clinical: Damage causes emotional and memory problems. Affected in Korsakoff syndrome.
Thal_MDm_L.funFact: This region connects your emotions to your decisions - it's where feelings meet thinking.

Thal_MDm_R.summary: Right mediodorsal medial thalamus for emotion-cognition integration.
Thal_MDm_R.function: Integrates emotional and cognitive information. Connects limbic system with frontal cortex. Important for emotional aspects of memory.
Thal_MDm_R.location: Medial mediodorsal thalamus of the right hemisphere.
Thal_MDm_R.clinical: Involved in mood disorders. Damage affects emotional processing.
Thal_MDm_R.funFact: When you make a gut feeling decision, information is likely flowing through this nucleus.

Thal_MGN_L.summary: Medial geniculate nucleus - gateway for hearing.
Thal_MGN_L.function: Relays auditory information from the brainstem to auditory cortex. Processes sound features like frequency and timing. Essential for hearing.
Thal_MGN_L.location: Posterior medial thalamus, part of the metathalamus.
Thal_MGN_L.clinical: Damage causes central hearing deficits. Important for auditory processing disorders.
Thal_MGN_L.funFact: The MGN can distinguish sounds just milliseconds apart - essential for understanding speech.

Thal_MGN_R.summary: Right medial geniculate nucleus for auditory processing.
Thal_MGN_R.function: Transmits and processes auditory information for the right hemisphere. Important for processing music and spatial aspects of sound.
Thal_MGN_R.location: Medial posterior thalamus of the right hemisphere.
Thal_MGN_R.clinical: Lesions affect sound localization and auditory processing.
Thal_MGN_R.funFact: Sound information reaches here in under 10 milliseconds - faster than you can consciously perceive.

Thal_PuA_L.function: Involved in visual attention and eye movements. Connects visual and parietal cortices. Important for filtering visual information.
Thal_PuA_L.location: Anterior portion of the pulvinar nucleus.
Thal_PuA_L.clinical: Damage affects visual attention. Important for understanding neglect.
Thal_PuA_L.funFact: The pulvinar helps you ignore irrelevant visual information - essential for focusing attention.

Thal_PuA_R.summary: Right anterior pulvinar for attention control.
Thal_PuA_R.function: Controls visual attention through connections with parietal and frontal cortex. Filters visual information based on relevance.
Thal_PuA_R.location: Anterior pulvinar of the right thalamus.
Thal_PuA_R.clinical: Involved in visual attention deficits. Important for spatial neglect.
Thal_PuA_R.funFact: When you search for something in a cluttered scene, the pulvinar helps spotlight the target.

Thal_PuI_L.summary: Inferior pulvinar for visual motion processing.
Thal_PuI_L.function: Processes visual motion and integrates visual information. Connects with motion-sensitive cortical areas. Important for tracking moving objects.
Thal_PuI_L.location: Inferior portion of the pulvinar nucleus.
Thal_PuI_L.clinical: Damage can affect motion perception. Important for visual processing disorders.
Thal_PuI_L.funFact: This region helps you track a moving ball or car - it's essential for perceiving motion.

Thal_PuI_R.summary: Right inferior pulvinar for motion processing.
Thal_PuI_R.function: Processes visual motion from the left visual field. Connects with motion-sensitive areas MT/V5. Important for smooth pursuit eye movements.
Thal_PuI_R.location: Inferior pulvinar of the right thalamus.
Thal_PuI_R.clinical: Lesions affect motion perception in the left visual field.
Thal_PuI_R.funFact: When you follow a moving target with your eyes, this region is guiding those smooth eye movements.

Thal_PuL_L.summary: Lateral pulvinar for higher visual processing.
Thal_PuL_L.function: Involved in higher-order visual processing and integration. Connects visual cortices with association areas. Important for object recognition.
Thal_PuL_L.location: Lateral portion of the pulvinar nucleus.
Thal_PuL_L.clinical: Damage affects visual recognition and integration. Important for visual agnosia.
Thal_PuL_L.funFact: The lateral pulvinar is massively expanded in humans, suggesting a role in our advanced visual cognition.

Thal_PuL_R.summary: Right lateral pulvinar for visual cognition.
Thal_PuL_R.function: Integrates visual information for higher cognition. Connects temporal and parietal visual areas. Important for complex visual processing.
Thal_PuL_R.location: Lateral pulvinar of the right thalamus.
Thal_PuL_R.clinical: Involved in complex visual processing deficits.
Thal_PuL_R.funFact: The pulvinar helps coordinate activity across distant visual areas - it's a visual traffic controller.

Thal_PuM_L.function: Integrates information from multiple sensory modalities. Connects with association cortices. Important for attention and multisensory processing.
Thal_PuM_L.location: Medial portion of the pulvinar nucleus.
Thal_PuM_L.clinical: Damage affects multisensory integration and attention.
Thal_PuM_L.funFact: This region helps combine what you see with what you hear into a unified perception.

Thal_PuM_R.summary: Right medial pulvinar for association functions.
Thal_PuM_R.function: Connects with association cortex for higher cognitive functions. Involved in multimodal integration and default mode network activity.
Thal_PuM_R.location: Medial pulvinar of the right thalamus.
Thal_PuM_R.clinical: Involved in disorders affecting higher cognitive functions.
Thal_PuM_R.funFact: The medial pulvinar is connected to the default mode network - it may play a role in mind-wandering.

Thal_Re_L.summary: Reticular thalamic nucleus for attention gating.
Thal_Re_L.function: Controls information flow through the thalamus. Acts as a gatekeeper determining what reaches cortex. Critical for selective attention.
Thal_Re_L.location: Shell-like structure surrounding the lateral thalamus.
Thal_Re_L.clinical: Dysfunction linked to attention disorders and epilepsy. Important for understanding attention mechanisms.
Thal_Re_L.funFact: Unlike other thalamic nuclei, this one doesn't project to cortex - it controls other thalamic nuclei.

Thal_Re_R.summary: Right reticular thalamus for thalamic gating.
Thal_Re_R.function: Inhibits and regulates thalamic relay nuclei. Controls attention by gating sensory information. Critical for sleep-wake regulation.
Thal_Re_R.location: Surrounds the right thalamus like a shell.
Thal_Re_R.clinical: Involved in absence seizures and attention disorders.
Thal_Re_R.funFact: During sleep, the reticular nucleus generates the sleep spindles that help consolidate memories.

Thal_VA_L.summary: Ventral anterior thalamus for motor and cognitive functions.
Thal_VA_L.function: Relays information from basal ganglia to frontal cortex. Involved in motor planning and cognitive functions. Part of frontal-subcortical circuits.
Thal_VA_L.location: Anterior portion of the ventral thalamus.
Thal_VA_L.clinical: Target for deep brain stimulation in movement disorders. Involved in cognitive aspects of Parkinson's.
Thal_VA_L.funFact: This nucleus helps translate your intentions into planned actions through its frontal connections.

Thal_VA_R.summary: Right ventral anterior thalamus for motor planning.
Thal_VA_R.function: Connects basal ganglia output with frontal cortex. Important for motor and cognitive functions. Part of parallel frontal-subcortical circuits.
Thal_VA_R.location: Ventral anterior thalamus of the right hemisphere.
Thal_VA_R.clinical: Involved in movement disorders and DBS targeting.
Thal_VA_R.funFact: Information from your basal ganglia must pass through here before reaching your frontal lobe for action.

Thal_VPL_L.summary: Ventral posterolateral thalamus for body sensation.
Thal_VPL_L.function: Relays somatosensory information from the body to cortex. Processes touch, pressure, pain, and temperature from contralateral body. Essential for tactile perception.
Thal_VPL_L.location: Ventral posterior thalamus, lateral to VPM.
Thal_VPL_L.clinical: Damage causes contralateral sensory loss. Thalamic pain syndrome can cause chronic pain.
Thal_VPL_L.funFact: All the touch information from your body must pass through this small region to reach consciousness.

Thal_VPL_R.summary: Right ventral posterolateral thalamus for left body sensation.
Thal_VPL_R.function: Transmits sensory information from left body to right somatosensory cortex. Processes fine touch, proprioception, and pain signals.
Thal_VPL_R.location: Ventral posterior thalamus of the right hemisphere.
Thal_VPL_R.clinical: Lesions cause left body numbness. Can develop into painful thalamic syndrome.
Thal_VPL_R.funFact: Stroke patients sometimes develop severe thalamic pain from damage here - the brain's relay gone wrong.

VTA_L.summary: Ventral tegmental area - reward and motivation center.
VTA_L.function: Major source of dopamine for reward and motivation circuits. Fires in response to unexpected rewards and reward predictions. Central to learning what's rewarding.
VTA_L.location: Midbrain, medial to the substantia nigra.
VTA_L.clinical: Dysfunction central to addiction and depression. Target for understanding reward-related disorders.
VTA_L.funFact: The VTA dopamine signal doesn't just respond to rewards - it signals when reality is better than expected.

VTA_R.summary: Right ventral tegmental area for reward signaling.
VTA_R.function: Releases dopamine to signal rewarding events and motivate behavior. Projects to prefrontal cortex and limbic system. Essential for reward learning.
VTA_R.location: Midbrain, right side of the ventral tegmentum.
VTA_R.clinical: Central to understanding addiction mechanisms. Target for depression research.
VTA_R.funFact: When something good happens unexpectedly, VTA neurons fire in a burst - that's the 'yes!' feeling.

Vermis_10.summary: Cerebellar vermis lobule X for vestibular processing.
Vermis_10.function: Processes vestibular information for balance and spatial orientation. Part of the vestibulocerebellum. Critical for eye-head coordination.
Vermis_10.location: Most inferior portion of the cerebellar vermis, nodulus.
Vermis_10.clinical: Damage causes severe balance problems and vertigo. Important for vestibular disorders.
Vermis_10.funFact: This tiny region keeps you from falling over - patients with damage here can barely stand.

Vermis_1_2.summary: Anterior vermis for basic motor coordination.
Vermis_1_2.function: Involved in basic motor coordination and postural control. Receives proprioceptive information from the body. Part of the spinocerebellum.
Vermis_1_2.location: Most anterior portion of the cerebellar vermis.
Vermis_1_2.clinical: Damage affects gait and posture. Early affected in alcoholic cerebellar degeneration.
Vermis_1_2.funFact: This ancient part of the cerebellum coordinates your basic ability to stand and walk.

Vermis_3.summary: Cerebellar vermis lobule III for trunk control.
Vermis_3.function: Controls trunk and proximal limb coordination. Receives input from the spinal cord. Important for maintaining posture.
Vermis_3.clinical: Damage causes truncal ataxia - difficulty with sitting and standing balance.
Vermis_3.funFact: Even when you're sitting still, this region is constantly adjusting your trunk to keep you balanced.

Vermis_4_5.summary: Vermis lobules IV-V for upper body coordination.
Vermis_4_5.function: Coordinates upper body and arm movements. Integrates proprioceptive feedback. Part of the intermediate cerebellum.
Vermis_4_5.location: Superior vermis, between anterior and posterior lobes.
Vermis_4_5.clinical: Lesions cause limb ataxia and coordination problems.
Vermis_4_5.funFact: This region contains a second body map in the cerebellum, complementing the one in the hemispheres.

Vermis_6.summary: Vermis lobule VI for oculomotor and limb coordination.
Vermis_6.function: Involved in eye movements and limb coordination. Part of the oculomotor vermis. Important for gaze control.
Vermis_6.clinical: Damage affects smooth pursuit eye movements and saccade accuracy.
Vermis_6.funFact: When your eyes smoothly follow a moving target, the vermis is fine-tuning those movements.

Vermis_7.summary: Vermis lobule VII for cognitive cerebellum.
Vermis_7.function: Involved in cognitive functions including language and working memory. Part of the cognitive cerebellum. Connects with prefrontal cortex.
Vermis_7.location: Posterior vermis, between lobules VI and VIII.
Vermis_7.clinical: Damage can cause cognitive deficits. Implicated in autism spectrum conditions.
Vermis_7.funFact: This part of the cerebellum is involved in thinking, not movement - a relatively recent discovery.

Vermis_8.summary: Vermis lobule VIII for sensorimotor integration.
Vermis_8.function: Integrates sensory and motor information. Part of the default mode cerebellar network. Involved in body awareness.
Vermis_8.clinical: Involved in cerebellar contributions to non-motor functions.
Vermis_8.funFact: Recent research shows this region is active during mind-wandering, connecting cerebellum to consciousness.

Vermis_9.summary: Vermis lobule IX for vestibular and default mode functions.
Vermis_9.function: Processes vestibular information and contributes to default mode network. Part of the posterior vermis. Involved in self-referential processing.
Vermis_9.clinical: Abnormalities linked to autism and social cognition deficits.
Vermis_9.funFact: This region connects with brain networks for self-reflection - the cerebellum may play a role in self-awareness.
